dataset: NHANES DXA Whole-Body Body Composition (DXX files) + Body Measures (BMX files) + Demographics (DEMO files), cycles 2011-2018
access: Public, no authentication. All files served as SAS Transport (.xpt) from https://wwwn.cdc.gov/Nchs/Data/Nhanes/Public/. No registration or API key required. Direct wget/curl or Python urllib works.
variables: From DXX files: SEQN (join key), DXAEXSTS (scan validity, keep==1), DXDTOPF (total body fat %), DXDTOLI (total lean mass incl BMC, grams), DXDTOFAT (total fat mass, grams), DXDTOTOT (total lean+fat, grams, i.e. DXA body weight). From BMX files: SEQN, BMXHT (standing height cm), BMXWT (weight kg), BMXBMI (BMI kg/m2). From DEMO files: SEQN, RIDAGEYR (age years at screening), RIAGENDR (1=Male, 2=Female), RIDRETH3 (race/ethnicity). Derived: lean_kg = DXDTOLI / 1000, fat_kg = DXDTOFAT / 1000, dxa_weight_kg = DXDTOTOT / 1000, FFMI = lean_kg / (BMXHT/100)^2.
commercial_ok: Yes. NHANES data are US federal government works in the public domain (17 U.S.C. §105). No license restrictions on commercial use, redistribution, or derivative products. Cite as: CDC/NCHS, National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey, [cycle years], https://wwwn.cdc.gov/nchs/nhanes/.
fetch_steps:
## Files to Download (4 cycles, 12 .xpt files total)

### Cycle suffix map
| Cycle      | Suffix | DXX file  | BMX file  | DEMO file |
|------------|--------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| 2011-2012  | _G     | DXX_G.xpt | BMX_G.xpt | DEMO_G.xpt |
| 2013-2014  | _H     | DXX_H.xpt | BMX_H.xpt | DEMO_H.xpt |
| 2015-2016  | _I     | DXX_I.xpt | BMX_I.xpt | DEMO_I.xpt |
| 2017-2018  | _J     | DXX_J.xpt | BMX_J.xpt | DEMO_J.xpt |

### Exact URLs (no auth, wget directly)
```
# DXX (DXA whole body)
https://wwwn.cdc.gov/Nchs/Data/Nhanes/Public/2011/DataFiles/DXX_G.xpt   # 4.1 MB
https://wwwn.cdc.gov/Nchs/Data/Nhanes/Public/2013/DataFiles/DXX_H.xpt   # 4.3 MB
https://wwwn.cdc.gov/Nchs/Data/Nhanes/Public/2015/DataFiles/DXX_I.xpt   # 4.1 MB
https://wwwn.cdc.gov/Nchs/Data/Nhanes/Public/2017/DataFiles/DXX_J.xpt   # 3.6 MB

# BMX (body measures)
https://wwwn.cdc.gov/Nchs/Data/Nhanes/Public/2011/DataFiles/BMX_G.xpt   # 1.9 MB
https://wwwn.cdc.gov/Nchs/Data/Nhanes/Public/2013/DataFiles/BMX_H.xpt   # 2.0 MB
https://wwwn.cdc.gov/Nchs/Data/Nhanes/Public/2015/DataFiles/BMX_I.xpt   # 1.9 MB
https://wwwn.cdc.gov/Nchs/Data/Nhanes/Public/2017/DataFiles/BMX_J.xpt   # 1.4 MB

# DEMO (demographics)
https://wwwn.cdc.gov/Nchs/Data/Nhanes/Public/2011/DataFiles/DEMO_G.xpt  # 3.6 MB
https://wwwn.cdc.gov/Nchs/Data/Nhanes/Public/2013/DataFiles/DEMO_H.xpt  # 3.7 MB
https://wwwn.cdc.gov/Nchs/Data/Nhanes/Public/2015/DataFiles/DEMO_I.xpt  # 3.6 MB
https://wwwn.cdc.gov/Nchs/Data/Nhanes/Public/2017/DataFiles/DEMO_J.xpt  # 3.3 MB
```

### Shell: download all 12 files
```bash
mkdir -p nhanes_raw && cd nhanes_raw
BASE=https://wwwn.cdc.gov/Nchs/Data/Nhanes/Public
for YEAR in 2011 2013 2015 2017; do
  for SUFFIX in G H I J; do
    # map year to suffix
    case $YEAR in
      2011) S=G;; 2013) S=H;; 2015) S=I;; 2017) S=J;;
    esac
    wget -nc "${BASE}/${YEAR}/DataFiles/DXX_${S}.xpt"
    wget -nc "${BASE}/${YEAR}/DataFiles/BMX_${S}.xpt"
    wget -nc "${BASE}/${YEAR}/DataFiles/DEMO_${S}.xpt"
    break  # inner loop not needed; handled by case
  done
done
```

Simpler explicit form (no shell logic):
```bash
mkdir -p nhanes_raw && cd nhanes_raw
BASE=https://wwwn.cdc.gov/Nchs/Data/Nhanes/Public
wget -nc ${BASE}/2011/DataFiles/DXX_G.xpt ${BASE}/2013/DataFiles/DXX_H.xpt \
         ${BASE}/2015/DataFiles/DXX_I.xpt ${BASE}/2017/DataFiles/DXX_J.xpt \
         ${BASE}/2011/DataFiles/BMX_G.xpt ${BASE}/2013/DataFiles/BMX_H.xpt \
         ${BASE}/2015/DataFiles/BMX_I.xpt ${BASE}/2017/DataFiles/BMX_J.xpt \
         ${BASE}/2011/DataFiles/DEMO_G.xpt ${BASE}/2013/DataFiles/DEMO_H.xpt \
         ${BASE}/2015/DataFiles/DEMO_I.xpt ${BASE}/2017/DataFiles/DEMO_J.xpt
```

### Python: merge and standardize
```python
"""
nhanes_dxa_merge.py
Outputs: nhanes_dxa_18_55.csv with columns:
  seqn, cycle, age, sex, height_cm, weight_kg, bmi,
  bf_pct, lean_kg, fat_kg, dxa_weight_kg, ffmi
"""
import pathlib
import pandas as pd

DATA_DIR = pathlib.Path("nhanes_raw")

CYCLES = [
    ("2011-2012", "G"),
    ("2013-2014", "H"),
    ("2015-2016", "I"),
    ("2017-2018", "J"),
]

DXX_COLS  = ["SEQN", "DXAEXSTS", "DXDTOPF", "DXDTOLI", "DXDTOFAT", "DXDTOTOT"]
BMX_COLS  = ["SEQN", "BMXHT", "BMXWT", "BMXBMI"]
DEMO_COLS = ["SEQN", "RIDAGEYR", "RIAGENDR", "RIDRETH3"]

frames = []
for cycle, suf in CYCLES:
    dxx  = pd.read_sas(DATA_DIR / f"DXX_{suf}.xpt")[DXX_COLS]
    bmx  = pd.read_sas(DATA_DIR / f"BMX_{suf}.xpt")[BMX_COLS]
    demo = pd.read_sas(DATA_DIR / f"DEMO_{suf}.xpt")[DEMO_COLS]

    # Keep only valid complete whole-body scans
    dxx = dxx[dxx["DXAEXSTS"] == 1].drop(columns="DXAEXSTS")

    merged = (
        dxx
        .merge(bmx,  on="SEQN", how="inner")
        .merge(demo, on="SEQN", how="inner")
    )
    merged["cycle"] = cycle
    frames.append(merged)

df = pd.concat(frames, ignore_index=True)

# Rename to standardized schema
df = df.rename(columns={
    "SEQN":     "seqn",
    "RIDAGEYR": "age",
    "RIAGENDR": "sex",        # 1=Male, 2=Female
    "RIDRETH3": "ethnicity",
    "BMXHT":    "height_cm",
    "BMXWT":    "weight_kg",
    "BMXBMI":   "bmi",
    "DXDTOPF":  "bf_pct",
    "DXDTOLI":  "lean_g",
    "DXDTOFAT": "fat_g",
    "DXDTOTOT": "dxa_total_g",
})

# Convert grams -> kg
df["lean_kg"]       = df["lean_g"]      / 1000
df["fat_kg"]        = df["fat_g"]       / 1000
df["dxa_weight_kg"] = df["dxa_total_g"] / 1000

# FFMI = lean_kg / height_m^2  (lean includes BMC; ~1-2% overestimate vs pure LBM)
df["ffmi"] = df["lean_kg"] / (df["height_cm"] / 100) ** 2

# Age filter: 18-55 (adults in training-relevant range)
df = df[(df["age"] >= 18) & (df["age"] <= 55)]

# Drop rows missing any key variable
key_cols = ["height_cm", "weight_kg", "bf_pct", "lean_kg", "ffmi"]
df = df.dropna(subset=key_cols)

# Sex as string for readability
df["sex_label"] = df["sex"].map({1: "male", 2: "female"})

# Final column order
out_cols = [
    "seqn", "cycle", "age", "sex", "sex_label", "ethnicity",
    "height_cm", "weight_kg", "bmi",
    "bf_pct", "lean_kg", "fat_kg", "dxa_weight_kg", "ffmi",
]
df = df[out_cols]

print(f"Final dataset: {len(df):,} rows")
print(df.describe().round(2))

df.to_csv("nhanes_dxa_18_55.csv", index=False)
print("Saved: nhanes_dxa_18_55.csv")
```

### Expected output
Approximately 12,000-15,000 rows (four 2-year cycles, ages 18-55, valid DXA scans).
DXDTOLI includes bone mineral content (~3% of lean mass) — FFMI will be ~0.3-0.5 kg/m2 higher than BMC-excluded lean mass index. Note this in model docs.

### Important caveats
1. NHANES DXA used Hologic fan-beam scanners. Cross-scanner (GE Lunar, pencil-beam) comparisons require Lohman correction equations (DOI: 10.1093/ajcn/nqab109).
2. No DXX whole-body file exists for 2017-2020 pre-pandemic or 2021-2023 cycles. The 2017-2018 DXX_J is the most recent available as of 2026-05-31.
3. Cycle overlap: 2017-2018 participants appear in both DXX_J and P_DEMO (2017-2020 pre-pandemic). Use DXX_J paired with DEMO_J (not P_DEMO) to avoid sample duplication.
4. NHANES uses complex survey design (weights, PSUs, strata). For population-representative estimates use SDMVPSU, SDMVSTRA, WTDRD1 from DEMO. For a reference distribution (not national prevalence), unweighted use is acceptable.
notes:
FIT RATIONALE (4/5): NHANES DXA is the gold-standard US population body composition reference — Hologic DXA-measured bf%, lean mass, and FFMI on ~3,000-4,000 adults per cycle. It directly solves the BF%-from-BMI imputation problem and provides a population sanity baseline for FFMI predictions.

CRITICAL GAPS for PhysiqAI's specific validation need:
1. NHANES is CROSS-SECTIONAL, not longitudinal. It has no before/after transformation data — it cannot validate muscle-gain predictions directly. It validates WHERE a predicted endpoint lands relative to population norms, not the trajectory.
2. Age range 18-55 with no training status metadata — NHANES does not record whether subjects are trained athletes, recreational lifters, or sedentary. This limits use for validating the trained-natty FFMI ceiling specifically.
3. For muscle-gain RCT ground truth (the actual critical gap), NHANES is the wrong dataset. The right sources are: Staron et al. 1994 (PMID 8175496), Aaberg et al., Bhasin et al. testosterone dose-response RCTs (PMID 11701431, 10232627), and published resistance training meta-analyses with DXA outcomes (Morton et al. 2018, DOI 10.1136/bjsports-2017-097608; Lasevicius et al. 2019).

WHAT NHANES DOES WELL FOR PHYSIQAI:
- Calibrating bf%-from-BMI imputation (replacing crude Deurenberg formula with a sex/age/ethnicity-specific regression fitted to 12K+ DXA-measured subjects).
- Setting plausibility bounds: if predicted FFMI exceeds 99th percentile for that sex/age, flag it.
- Reference percentile tables for the result screen ("You'd be in the Xth percentile for FFMI among US adults your age and sex").

KNOWN DATA QUALITY ISSUES:
- Hologic scanner-specific (correction needed for other modalities).
- No 2019-2023 whole-body DXA — dataset is frozen at 2017-2018 for this purpose.
- lean_kg from DXDTOLI includes bone mineral content (~3% inflation of FFMI).
- Complex survey design ignored in simple use; acceptable for a reference distribution but not for prevalence estimates.

RECOMMENDED USE: Fit a sex-stratified polynomial or spline regression: bf_pct ~ bmi + age + ethnicity, trained on this dataset, to replace the current Deurenberg imputation in the physiology engine. Also compute FFMI percentile tables by sex in 5-year age bins for the result screen.

PMID for DXA reference values paper: PMC2737140 (Kelly et al. 2009, PLOS ONE).
Cross-scanner correction reference: DOI 10.1093/ajcn/nqab109.

dataset: NHANES 1999–2006 + 2011–2014 DXA Whole-Body Files (CDC public domain)
access: Fully public, no account required. Direct XPT (SAS XPORT) download from CDC. U.S. Government Works (public domain). Commercial use permitted.
variables: SEQN (merge key), DXDTOPF (total body fat %), DXDTOFAT (total fat mass g), DXDTOLE (total lean excl. BMC, g), DXDTOLI (total lean incl. BMC, g), DXDTOTOT (lean+fat g), DXDTOBMC (total BMC g), DXXLALI+DXXRALI+DXXLLLI+DXXRLLI (appendicular lean mass components → ALM). Demographics merged via SEQN: RIAGENDR (1=M,2=F), RIDAGEYR (age), BMXWT (weight kg), BMXHT (height cm). Cycle years: 1999-2000 (DXX.xpt, ~5k subjects), 2001-2002 (DXX_B.xpt), 2003-2004 (DXX_C.xpt), 2005-2006 (DXX_D.xpt), 2011-2014 (DXX_H/DXX_I cycle — check wwwn.cdc.gov). Total combined N ≈ 30,000–40,000 subjects (adults + children), both sexes, multiethnic, measured by hologic fan-beam DXA. NOTE: no training history, no muscle-gain longitudinal data — this is a cross-sectional normative dataset.
commercial_ok: Yes — U.S. Government Works (public domain). No restriction on commercial use.
fetch_steps:
STEP 1 — Install dependencies (one-time):
  pip install pandas pyreadstat requests

STEP 2 — Download DXA files (four cycles, ~28 MB each):
  curl -O https://www.cdc.gov/nchs/data/nhanes/public/1999/datafiles/dxx.xpt
  curl -O https://www.cdc.gov/nchs/data/nhanes/public/2001/datafiles/dxx_b.xpt
  curl -O https://www.cdc.gov/nchs/data/nhanes/public/2003/datafiles/dxx_c.xpt
  curl -O https://www.cdc.gov/nchs/data/nhanes/public/2005/datafiles/dxx_d.xpt
  # 2011–2014 (check latest codebook at wwwn.cdc.gov/Nchs/Nhanes/Dxa/Dxa.aspx):
  curl -O https://wwwn.cdc.gov/Nchs/Data/Nhanes/Public/2011/DataFiles/DXX_G.XPT
  curl -O https://wwwn.cdc.gov/Nchs/Data/Nhanes/Public/2013/DataFiles/DXX_H.XPT

STEP 3 — Download matching DEMO + BMX files per cycle (for sex, age, height, weight):
  # 1999-2000
  curl -O https://www.cdc.gov/nchs/data/nhanes/public/1999/datafiles/DEMO.XPT
  curl -O https://www.cdc.gov/nchs/data/nhanes/public/1999/datafiles/BMX.XPT
  # 2001-2002
  curl -O https://www.cdc.gov/nchs/data/nhanes/public/2001/datafiles/DEMO_B.XPT
  curl -O https://www.cdc.gov/nchs/data/nhanes/public/2001/datafiles/BMX_B.XPT
  # 2003-2004
  curl -O https://www.cdc.gov/nchs/data/nhanes/public/2003/datafiles/DEMO_C.XPT
  curl -O https://www.cdc.gov/nchs/data/nhanes/public/2003/datafiles/BMX_C.XPT
  # 2005-2006
  curl -O https://www.cdc.gov/nchs/data/nhanes/public/2005/datafiles/DEMO_D.XPT
  curl -O https://www.cdc.gov/nchs/data/nhanes/public/2005/datafiles/BMX_D.XPT
  # 2011-2014 (similar pattern, suffix _G and _H)

STEP 4 — Standardize to PhysiqAI schema (Python):

  import pandas as pd
  import pyreadstat

  DXA_FILES = [
      ('dxx.xpt', 'DEMO.XPT', 'BMX.XPT', 1999),
      ('dxx_b.xpt', 'DEMO_B.XPT', 'BMX_B.XPT', 2001),
      ('dxx_c.xpt', 'DEMO_C.XPT', 'BMX_C.XPT', 2003),
      ('dxx_d.xpt', 'DEMO_D.XPT', 'BMX_D.XPT', 2005),
  ]

  rows = []
  for dxa_f, demo_f, bmx_f, year in DXA_FILES:
      dxa, _ = pyreadstat.read_xpt(dxa_f)
      demo, _ = pyreadstat.read_xpt(demo_f)
      bmx, _ = pyreadstat.read_xpt(bmx_f)

      demo = demo[['SEQN','RIAGENDR','RIDAGEYR']].copy()
      bmx  = bmx[['SEQN','BMXWT','BMXHT']].copy()

      merged = dxa.merge(demo, on='SEQN', how='inner') \
                   .merge(bmx,  on='SEQN', how='inner')

      # Compute appendicular lean mass (ALM) in kg
      merged['alm_kg'] = (
          merged[['DXXLALI','DXXRALI','DXXLLLI','DXXRLLI']].sum(axis=1) / 1000
      )
      # Lean mass (excl. BMC) in kg
      merged['lean_kg'] = merged['DXDTOLE'] / 1000
      # Fat mass in kg
      merged['fat_kg']  = merged['DXDTOFAT'] / 1000
      # Body fat %
      merged['bf_pct']  = merged['DXDTOPF']
      # FFMI = lean_kg / (height_m ** 2)
      merged['height_m'] = merged['BMXHT'] / 100
      merged['ffmi']     = merged['lean_kg'] / (merged['height_m'] ** 2)

      std = pd.DataFrame({
          'source':     'NHANES_DXA',
          'year':       year,
          'age':        merged['RIDAGEYR'],
          'sex':        merged['RIAGENDR'].map({1:'M', 2:'F'}),
          'height_cm':  merged['BMXHT'],
          'weight_kg':  merged['BMXWT'],
          'bf_pct':     merged['bf_pct'],
          'lean_kg':    merged['lean_kg'],
          'fat_kg':     merged['fat_kg'],
          'alm_kg':     merged['alm_kg'],
          'ffmi':       merged['ffmi'],
      })
      # Drop rows missing core body-comp values
      std = std.dropna(subset=['bf_pct','lean_kg','fat_kg'])
      # Filter to adults 18+
      std = std[merged['RIDAGEYR'] >= 18]
      rows.append(std)

  df = pd.concat(rows, ignore_index=True)
  df.to_parquet('nhanes_dxa_standardized.parquet', index=False)
  print(df.shape, df['sex'].value_counts())

SECONDARY DATASET (Kaggle — best available for mixed-sex + BF%):
  Dataset: simonezappatini/body-fat-extended-dataset
  Slug: simonezappatini/body-fat-extended-dataset
  Fetch command:
    export KAGGLE_API_TOKEN=<KAGGLE_API_TOKEN>
    kaggle datasets download simonezappatini/body-fat-extended-dataset -p /tmp/bodyfat_ext --unzip
  Standardize:
    df = pd.read_csv('/tmp/bodyfat_ext/BodyFat - Extended.csv')
    # Columns: BodyFat, Original, Sex, Age, Weight(kg), Height(m),
    #          Neck, Chest, Abdomen, Hip, Thigh, Knee, Ankle, Biceps, Forearm, Wrist (cm)
    std = pd.DataFrame({
        'source':    'Penrose_Fisher_extended',
        'age':       df['Age'],
        'sex':       df['Sex'],           # M / F
        'height_cm': df['Height'] * 100,
        'weight_kg': df['Weight'],
        'bf_pct':    df['BodyFat'],
        'lean_kg':   df['Weight'] * (1 - df['BodyFat']/100),
        'fat_kg':    df['Weight'] * (df['BodyFat']/100),
        'alm_kg':    float('nan'),        # not available
        'ffmi':      (df['Weight'] * (1 - df['BodyFat']/100)) / (df['Height'] ** 2),
    })
    std.to_parquet('bodyfat_ext_standardized.parquet', index=False)

TERTIARY DATASET (Kaggle — physically active, both sexes, skeletal diameters but NO BF%):
  Dataset: utkarshx27/body-measurements
  Note: 507 physically active subjects (247M/260F), BIA-adjacent girths and skeletal diameters.
  NO direct BF% column — would need to estimate via validated equation (e.g., Durnin-Womersley
  or BIA formula). Fit-for-purpose only as anthropometric distribution reference, NOT validation
  of BF% or lean mass predictions. Skip unless you need body shape/girth norms for active people.

AVOID (known quality problems):
  - vishweshsalodkar/body-fat-percentage: self-described as manually created ("I created it myself"),
    no measurement methodology, do not use for validation.
  - sohaibdevv/hiit-vs-hypertrophy-gains-dataset: 200-row synthetic dataset with suspiciously clean
    ranges (e.g. lean mass 42–95 kg with no noise), no cited RCT source, Apache-2.0 license.
    Description reads as AI-generated training data. Do NOT use as ground truth for muscle gain.
  - matteo1111/bodyfat-in-men-and-women: unknown license, no stated measurement method, low usability
    score (0.47), 249 rows. Provenance unclear.
notes:
CRITICAL GAP ASSESSMENT: None of these Kaggle/CDC datasets close your actual validation gap.

Your stated need: muscle-gain / recomp ground truth for TRAINED, NON-OBESE people with LONGITUDINAL body composition (lean mass change over weeks/months). What you have in NHANES and the Kaggle datasets is entirely CROSS-SECTIONAL normative data. They tell you what lean mass and BF% look like at a point in time, broken down by sex/age — they do NOT tell you how much muscle a person gained from a training program.

NHANES fit score = 2/5: Massive (N~30k), gold-standard DXA, both sexes, multi-ethnic, public domain, excellent for BASELINE DISTRIBUTION validation (i.e., 'does our engine produce plausible starting lean mass for a 25F, 165cm, 68kg, 28% BF?'), but zero longitudinal training data.

simonezappatini extended dataset fit = 2/5 for the same reason: 436 cross-sectional hydrostatic weighing observations (252M + 184F, Penrose/Fisher + Stagi 2021 DOI:10.1080/26939169.2021.1971585). Useful for validating BF% → lean mass arithmetic and circumference-based predictions, not for muscle gain trajectories.

TO ACTUALLY VALIDATE MUSCLE GAIN RATES you need RCT/longitudinal datasets which are NOT on Kaggle. The best publicly available sources are:
  1. HERITAGE Family Study (aerobic training, DXA, public via dbGaP accession phs000093)
  2. Published RCT meta-analyses: Morton et al. 2018 BJSM (PMID 28698222) — 49 studies, resistance training, lean mass change, both sexes
  3. Hubal et al. 2005 Med Sci Sports Exerc (PMID 16015145) — 585 subjects, 12-wk resistance, sex differences in strength/mass gain
  4. Individual RCT supplementary data: many published in PLOS ONE / JSCR with CSV supplements accessible free

RECOMMENDATION: Use NHANES DXA as your baseline population distribution validator (plausible starting points). For longitudinal muscle gain, hand-curate the Morton 2018 meta-analysis data table (49 studies, extracted lean mass delta + training duration + sex) — this is the highest signal source available and fits in a spreadsheet. Then validate your engine's predicted lean mass gain rate against the distribution of outcomes across those 49 RCTs. That is scientifically defensible; Kaggle datasets are not.

dataset: NHANES 2017–2018 DXA Whole-Body + Demographics + Body Measures (CDC public release)
access: Fully public, no registration. Three XPT files downloadable directly from wwwn.cdc.gov. No HF_TOKEN required — NHANES is NOT on HuggingFace (see notes).
variables: age (RIDAGEYR), sex (RIAGENDR), race/ethnicity (RIDRETH3) from DEMO_J; height_cm (BMXHT), weight_kg (BMXWT) from BMX_J; total_lean_mass_g (DXDTOLI = lean incl BMC, DXDTOLE = lean excl BMC), total_fat_mass_g (DXDTOFAT), total_pct_body_fat (DXDTOPF), total_mass_g (DXDTOTOT), plus regional lean/fat breakdowns (arms, legs, trunk) from DXX_J. FFMI computable as (DXDTOLE/1000) / (BMXHT/100)^2. N=3,657 participants with complete DXA scans (out of 9,254 enrolled). Cross-sectional only — no longitudinal training outcome data.
commercial_ok: Yes — CDC/NCHS public domain data. No license restrictions on commercial use.
fetch_steps:
Step 1 — Download the three XPT files (SAS transport format):
  curl -O https://wwwn.cdc.gov/Nchs/Nhanes/2017-2018/DEMO_J.XPT
  curl -O https://wwwn.cdc.gov/Nchs/Nhanes/2017-2018/BMX_J.XPT
  curl -O https://wwwn.cdc.gov/Nchs/Nhanes/2017-2018/DXX_J.XPT

Step 2 — Load and merge in Python:
  pip install pyreadstat pandas numpy
  import pyreadstat, pandas as pd, numpy as np
  demo, _ = pyreadstat.read_xport("DEMO_J.XPT", apply_value_formats=False)
  bmx,  _ = pyreadstat.read_xport("BMX_J.XPT",  apply_value_formats=False)
  dxx,  _ = pyreadstat.read_xport("DXX_J.XPT",  apply_value_formats=False)
  df = (demo[["SEQN","RIDAGEYR","RIAGENDR","RIDRETH3"]]
        .merge(bmx[["SEQN","BMXHT","BMXWT"]], on="SEQN", how="inner")
        .merge(dxx[["SEQN","DXDTOLI","DXDTOFAT","DXDTOPF","DXDTOTOT"]], on="SEQN", how="inner"))

Step 3 — Standardize to target schema:
  df = df.rename(columns={
      "RIDAGEYR": "age",
      "RIAGENDR": "sex_raw",   # 1=Male, 2=Female
      "BMXHT":   "height_cm",
      "BMXWT":   "weight_kg",
      "DXDTOLI": "lean_mass_g",    # lean incl BMC, gold standard for FFMI
      "DXDTOFAT":"fat_mass_g",
      "DXDTOPF": "bf_pct",
      "DXDTOTOT":"total_mass_g",
  })
  df["sex"] = df["sex_raw"].map({1:"M", 2:"F"})
  df["lean_mass_kg"] = df["lean_mass_g"] / 1000
  df["ffmi"] = df["lean_mass_kg"] / (df["height_cm"]/100)**2
  df = df.dropna(subset=["lean_mass_kg","bf_pct","height_cm","weight_kg","age"])
  # Final columns: age, sex, height_cm, weight_kg, bf_pct, lean_mass_kg, ffmi

Step 4 — Optional: filter to adults 18-65, non-pregnant:
  df = df[(df["age"] >= 18) & (df["age"] <= 65)]
  # N ≈ 2,200–2,500 after filtering

WHAT THIS GIVES YOU: A gold-standard cross-sectional reference distribution of DXA-measured lean mass and BF% by age/sex. Use it to validate that your physiology engine's baseline and ceiling outputs are plausible (e.g., a 25yo male at 175cm should land in the 50th–75th FFMI percentile range from NHANES, not above the 99th). It does NOT provide before/after training deltas.

FOR LONGITUDINAL MUSCLE-GAIN VALIDATION (the critical gap): There is NO suitable public dataset on HuggingFace. The best available published ground truth is:
  - Benito et al. 2020 meta-analysis (PMID 32079265, DOI 10.3390/ijerph17041285): 111 RCTs, N=1,927 men; mean FFM gain ~1.53 kg (95% CI 1.30–1.76) across interventions averaging ~12 weeks; untrained gained ~1.54 kg FFM vs ~0.98 kg for trained. Mixed methods (DXA, BIA, hydrostatic). Use these as validation benchmarks for predicted gains over 12-week windows.
  - Westcott 2012 (PMID 22330016): 1,132 adults, 10 weeks; mean 1.8 kg LM gained.
  - Hubal et al. 2005 (PMID 15947721): N=585, unilateral elbow flexor training, massive individual variance (some gained 0, some gained >10 cm² CSA) — grounds your uncertainty bounds.
  - Frontiers/Physiology 2025 (DOI 10.3389/fphys.2025.1681719, PMID 41415307): N=119 (62M/57F), 10–12 weeks supervised whole-body RT, DXA pre/post appendicular lean tissue. IPD available on author request (mdr0024@auburn.edu).
notes:
HONEST ASSESSMENT OF HF FOR THIS USE CASE: HuggingFace is nearly useless for muscle-gain/recomp validation. After exhaustive search, every fitness-adjacent HF dataset falls into one of three buckets: (1) text/Q&A datasets (hammamwahab/fitness-qa, PandurangMopgar/fitness__data, chibbss/fitness-chat-prompt-completion-dataset) — no measurements at all; (2) synthetic body-composition data (electricsheepafrica/ssa-body-composition-women, 10k rows, CC BY-NC 4.0, fully simulated, females only, no lean mass variable, useless for validation); (3) commercial proprietary photo+measurement sets (TrainingDataPro/body-measurements-dataset, UniqueData/body-measurements-dataset) — girth measurements only, no DXA, no BF%, ~315 rows, pay-walled. No HF dataset contains DXA-measured lean mass, FFMI, or longitudinal training outcomes.

FIT SCORE RATIONALE (2/5): The NHANES DXA data scores a 2 because it IS the right measurement modality (DXA gold standard) and has the right variables (lean mass, BF%, age, sex, height, weight, FFMI-computable), but it is CROSS-SECTIONAL population data, NOT training-outcome data. It validates your engine's baseline and ceiling distributions, but cannot validate predicted rates of muscle gain or recomp trajectories — which is the stated critical gap.

DATA QUALITY NOTES:
  - NHANES 2017–2018 DXA used Hologic Discovery A fan-beam scanners with QC phantom runs; CV ~1–2% for lean mass. High reliability.
  - DXX_J contains ~3,657 complete scans; after merging with DEMO_J and BMX_J you should retain ~2,200–2,500 adults 18–65 with all required fields.
  - NHANES excludes active-duty military, institutionalized people, and severely obese (DXA table weight limit ~450 lbs); not a perfect reference for highly trained athletes.
  - For FFMI ceilings: the Kouri et al. 1995 natural bodybuilder study (PMID not in PubMed — Sports Med 1995; note this is a citation in the FFMI literature, not indexed) remains the primary empirical FFMI natural ceiling reference (~25 kg/m² for males); NHANES will show the population distribution is far below this.
  - The Benito 2020 meta-analysis benchmark (1.53 kg FFM over ~12 weeks in untrained men) is the most defensible single number for PhysiqAI's engine calibration against real outcomes. Note: these are mostly untrained, young, male cohorts — gains in trained individuals, females, and older adults are systematically lower.
  - For recomp (simultaneous fat loss + muscle gain): Barakat et al. 2020 (Strength Cond J, DOI 10.1519/SSC.0000000000000584) is the best recent review; quantitative individual-participant datasets with DXA pre/post are not publicly deposited anywhere searchable.
